column4: Do you want a common clock for reading and writing the FIFO?
column6:
"0: Yes, synchronize both reading and writing to \'clock\'.
Create one set of full/empty control signals." "4: No, synchronize reading and writing to \'rdclk\' and \'wrclk\', respectively.
Create a set of full/empty control signals for each clock."

"0: No, the clocks are not synchronized, add latency to handle
unrelated clocks. (Note: The last three words of the FIFO may not
be available for writing because of the synchronization pipelines
between the two clock domains." "1:Yes, the clocks are synchronized, do not add clock
synchronization latency. (Note: Clocks must be synchronized
and must be integer multiples of each other.)"

column4: Which type of optimization do you want?
column6:
"3: Lowest latency but requires synchronized clocks
1 sync stage, no metastability protection, smallest size, good fmax." "4: Minimal setting for unsynchronized clocks
2 sync stages, good metastability protection, medium size, good fmax." "5:Best metastability protection, best fmax, unsynchronized clocks
3 or more sync stages, best metastability protection, largest size, best fmax."

column4: Which kind of read access do you want with the 'rdreq' signal?
column6:
"1: Normal synchronous FIFO mode.
The data becomes available after 'rdreq' is asserted.
'rdreq' acts as a read request." "0: Show-ahead synchronous FIFO mode.
The data becomes available before 'rdreq' is asserted.
'rdreq' acts as a read acknowledge.
Note: This mode suffers a performance penalty."
